channel.title: Comments for page "New Pages Feed"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "New Pages Feed"

item.title: GoI-Formats
item.content:encoded: I definitely agree with TheJonyMister that tales and GoI Formats should be separated as it really is annoying not knowing if that article is one of the four tales guaranteed to be posted today or a rare GOI-Format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is coming from someone who doesn't know how hard this is to make or fix or design, but I cant say I like pushing the tales and GOI formats together, since its not really obvious which its going to be from the title. Becomes a problem when I'm hunting for the latest GOI formats and I have to sift through all the tales. Once again, not sure of another way to do this besides two more rows a screen and a half down (which is inadvisable, I imagine), but I'm saying this sort of to document that it's something that I and possibly others may be concerned about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The changes are a step in the right direction, and barring any changes in how tag team works or a new platform, this seems close to ideal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Untagged pages are now in both the top and bottom, to serve as an asset for any staff member looking to tag them without removing them from the top-level feed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I was unclear. To clarify, I imagine that new users would try out both modules and prefer the one that has tagged, positively rated articles in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the MRC page was always a list of meaningless numbers. The way to get around this would be to display titles and maybe a short blurb, but as far as I know wikidot doesn't allow this easily.

Wouldn't a new reader not realise the ratings of articles in each module? You have to click through to see the ratings. I think the most recent update at least makes the tales a little more visible by pushing them towards the top of the page, and I don't think anything is lost from the previous MRC page, though it's not ideal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Splitting MRC into one list of nearly meaningless numbers and one list that doesn't work as intended is not an improvement.

Apparently this was done to improve Tale visibility? As a new reader I'd probably use the module that barely has any negatively rated articles in it, not the one with a bunch of nonstandard titles and no clear structure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My biggest core issue with this page is that it benefits users who have tagged their pages, or have had their pages tagged for them, versus those who don't. That still benefits SCPs on the whole, and while separating SCPs from other pages manages to give other types of articles slight boosts in visibility, there still won't be much in the way of that simply by nature of skips being the format people write in the most. It also results in the feed changing and shifting a lot more than usual, being that its 1) tagged-based and 2) tags can get added anywhere from seconds after posting to weeks1.

I think the way to go forward with this type of feed is to encourage people to tag new articles with just the format that the article is in, and let either tagging team or otherwise-knowledgeable tagging users handle the rest of the tags. This way, the feed won't experience irregular shifting due to tags being implemented much later, and it even allows for future expansion and design possibilites with the page.2

The biggest issue with this approach that I can find is that people who tag use the current chatbot to see what articles have gone untagged, and tag based off of that. It's an issue that could be solved, but I don't know the amount of work needed to make the bot distinguish between format tags and all other types of tags.

Footnotes
1. Much rarer than it used to be (and it was still very rare too), but it's happened enough that I'll point that out.
2. I also acknowledge that these issues inherently stem from opting to use modules that need tags to organize, which is definitely a platform limitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And actual clean and organized Web 2.0 designs are more usable, accessible, and intuitive than messy Web 1.0 designs. This page doesn't feel too usable or intuitive compared to the old one, but I don't think that has to do with Web 1.0 vs Web 2.0 stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I'm saying is

Messy Web 1.0 designs > "clean", "organized" Web 2.0 designs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the ideal webpage. You may not like it, but this is what peak performance looks like.
Seriously though, what the hell does 'I'm not a fan of organizing when it comes to web design" even mean????

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the mean time we could at least shorten the columns. Even in the SCPs column my article from early this month is still visible. Right now any person is only going to see the most recent SCPs and tales, if it's all visible in one screen at least untagged SCPs and GoI formats will have a chance.

Though personally I would argue the whole categorization by article type is poor design, since the primary function of this page is to show new pages. I would personally prefer if we merged all the categories together into one large feed, and then having something on the side display if it's tagged scp/tale/goi/etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amended based on feedback, specifically the below-the-fold issue for GoI Formats and Other pages has resulted in those three feeds being conglomerated into one feed, while the point of it not being the best on mobile will be addressed by a mobile version of the page being implemented in the not-too-distant-future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3 more boxes feels more like an attempt to organize and uh I'm not really a fan of organizing when it comes to web design

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't want to dig through my bookmarks for a page that takes an extra few clicks to get to on the website. It's inconvenient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure what you mean by this? It's just the same as the old one except 4 of them - could you elaborate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woahhh what the fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with these posts. Additionally, if you click past page 1 the formatting for the page number navigation bar breaks, at least in my browser (the arrows in front of previous and behind next move to different lines). Even on the base page the formatting is wonky (page 1 of / 103 are on separate lines).

Cutting down the number of lines displayed so you don't have to scroll so far would be a good start, but I don't know how you fix the other problems, particularly the non-tagged articles being dumped at the bottom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh, I'll always prefer the simple Web 1.5 (is there a more accurate name for web design of the 2000s that's not really web 1.0 and not 2.0?) esque design of the old new pages list. I can at least appreciate what this is doing but aesthetically it doesn't click with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While Cimmerian does make good points.
I'd say that change is an improvement.

Is it perfect? No, but then again, nothing really is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This format seriously benefits anyone who can tag their own works. Anyone that can't will likely have a hard time finding any traction since they're way way below the fold.

And with this setup I have no idea what the fix for this is. Even if you could fix the issue that SCPs are untagged for anywhere between 1-3 days (and sometimes more if something comes up or the workload increases), you'd still be pushing non-tale and non-scp works below the fold.

I mean you could roll everything into "SCP" or "Non-SCP" works. Most posted articles (I'd wager at least) are SCPs, after all, so rolling the other categories (like art pages, hubs, site-dossiers, tales, GOIs) into one section wouldn't be the worst thing in the world.

Any solution that places new creative works below the fold is a misstep, I think. People rely on this page to find new articles, and this has fundamentally altered how most site regulars (and not an insignificant portion of casual readers) interact with the site in a way I really am not a fan of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanted to weigh in and say I agree with all of stormbreaths points here, especially this one:

The easiest way to read through recently created SCPs and remember where you left off was by judging off location between non-SCP writing. I could look at the old list and say "I have read all SCPs between these two tales, with non SCP names. The only way to do this as it stands is through -EX and -Js, which are not particularly common.

This is primarily how I interact with the site and this has fundamentally changed that (I didn't realise the old page was still available, which I now see, but still.)

It also has another effect of separating SCP articles into two lists - tagged and untagged. There are obvious issues here, not least of which is that, after a quick browse back (which feels super weird, for the record - clicking a page number on one of the windows and having the entire page reload to have that single box update) shows that there are untagged SCPs as old as January 1st. Looks like this is from clichecon which explains how they were missed, but were this the active page at the time those articles would have probably completely missed out on getting any readers from Recently Created.

Additionally, people who don't tag their articles for whatever reason (which is the recommended practice) are going to be at a disadvantage, which will escalate depending on how long that tagging takes - less time at the top of the New SCP list means less readers, and if it takes a long time (days for example, which looking at the lists right now, isn't unreasonable) they are going to miss out pretty heavily as they are never going to see the top of that list. I certainly don't intend to be regularly scrolling down to that Untagged list, and I can't imagine I will be the only one.

I was vaguely following along with this on O5 and saw no issues with it at the time, but as stormbreath said; seeing this in action has made it apparent how unhelpful it is.

ETA: I just realised that navigating one page on one of the boxes changes the current page on all the boxes. I realise this is probably unavoidable on wikidot without some extensive javascripting on the page, but from a UI point of view this is pretty icky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poor GoI formats get shafted below their more popular cousins :(

(of course, it's impossible to fit all 3 on one line within the wikidot margins, but I enjoy having sympathy for an abstract concept nonetheless)

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that I've passed these comments on to the staff site, and I believe it should be easy to add a link to the old page (which, to be honest, I kind of prefer setup-wise).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Such a suggestion would mean the Tech Team would need to find a new way to find untagged SCPs, as the current method is to look for SCPs without any tags. This may be possible, but will increase work for staff.

Articles being tagged with a single tag means that the Tech Team will miss them. SCP-4600, SCP-4777, SCP-4591 and SCP-4079 have been untagged for seven months because a single tag was added.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new page dropped right as I was in the old page, and i got so confused the next time I pressed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just as a suggestion to help the untagged SCPs thing, just automatically tag all SCPs with SCP like with what happens with tales.

Edit: Also, just for the record, on mobile you have to scroll down a fair bit before you get to tales, let alone GoI Formats. If this is the format for New Pages, it might be good to give it the Top Rated Pages treatment and shrink down the windows for each one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing this in action makes this apparent how unhelpful this is. This isn't going to help visibility at all. Couple reasons:

  • The easiest way to read through recently created SCPs and remember where you left off was by judging off location between non-SCP writing. I could look at the old list and say "I have read all SCPs between these two tales, with non SCP names. The only way to do this as it stands is through -EX and -Js, which are not particularly common.
  • To get to GoI formats and other pages, you have to scroll past the entirety of the other two lists, which are fairly long. On mobile, you need to scroll past SCPs to get to tales, tales to get to GoIs and GoIs to get to other stuff. This makes content unnecessarily difficult to even see.
  • Untagged pages have been relegated to the bottom right corner, mixed in with any other page. This separates and buries new SCP articles. Are there plans to dramatically increase the rate at which SCPs are tagged, in order to facilitate this? This seems like it will act as an unnecessary burden on Tech, or at the very least tempt users into tagging their own works, which is not something Tech generally wants.
  • If I want to see what new articles have been posted since I last checked, I know need to visit four separate lists in turn, rather than a single one. A minor inconvenience, but still present.
  • There's also some general clunkiness that comes from have four page separated listpage modules on the same page - you have to go to the next page on every list if you want to see more articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can just bookmark the old page

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to agree with this. It doesn't necessarily look bad, but an article that I wrote in December is displayed under the "Recently Created GoI Formats" tab. I'd rather see articles made within the last week than everything "recently made".

I know that the old page still exists, but I'd rather have an option to be able to have an easy click to the old page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: roget you actually did it you madman

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not to be a contrarian but this honestly just make new pages harder to look for, i come to the "recently created pages" page to look at recently created pages, not like four windows.

it would be better if this was listed as an option on the original page imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me gusta

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is looking pretty slick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So when will the funeral ceremony for the old feed happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what th

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an update to categorize recently created pages, as voted on here

Original page can be found Here

Please note any performance issues or problems in this thread and they will be addressed. Thanks to Croque for helping me get this lookin' good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All hail the glorious new feed!

EDIT: The new page changes have effectively hidden new untagged articles, which is a definite step back for me and for new articles.
